name: Insertion Sort
definition: Insertion Sort builds a sorted array one element at a time by picking each element and inserting it into its correct position among previously sorted elements. It works similarly to sorting playing cards in hand.
intuition: Imagine sorting playing cards. You pick one card at a time and insert it into the correct position among cards already in your hand.
steps: Start from the second element (index 1), treat the first element as already sorted
Compare key with each element to its left
Shift all elements greater than key one position to the right
Insert key at the correct position
pseudocode:
INSERTION-SORT(A):
  for j = 2 to A.length
    key = A[j]
    i = j - 1
    while i > 0 and A[i] > key
      A[i+1] = A[i]
      i = i - 1
    A[i+1] = key
loop_invariant: At start of each iteration, subarray A[1..j-1] consists of elements originally in A[1..j-1] but in sorted order.
advantages: Efficient for small or nearly sorted arrays

comparisons.merge_sort: Insertion Sort is O(n²) vs Merge Sort O(n log n). Use Insertion Sort for small arrays (n < 20).
comparisons.bubble_sort: Both O(n²) but Insertion Sort makes fewer swaps and is faster in practice.
comparisons.selection_sort: Both O(n²) but Insertion Sort is stable and faster on nearly sorted data.

name: Merge Sort
definition: Merge Sort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that recursively splits an array into two halves, sorts each half, and merges them into a sorted array. It has guaranteed O(n log n) time complexity in all cases.
intuition: Split a deck of cards into two piles. Sort each pile separately. Then merge by always picking the smaller top card from either pile.
steps: DIVIDE: Find midpoint q = floor((p+r)/2) of array A[p..r]
CONQUER: Recursively sort left half A[p..q]
CONQUER: Recursively sort right half A[q+1..r]
COMBINE: Merge the two sorted halves using MERGE procedure
Base case: single element array is already sorted
pseudocode:
MERGE-SORT(A, p, r):
  if p < r
    q = floor((p+r)/2)
    MERGE-SORT(A, p, q)
    MERGE-SORT(A, q+1, r)
    MERGE(A, p, q, r)

MERGE(A, p, q, r):
  n1 = q-p+1, n2 = r-q
  Create L[1..n1+1] and R[1..n2+1]
  L[n1+1] = R[n2+1] = infinity
  i = j = 1
  for k = p to r:
    if L[i] <= R[j]: A[k]=L[i], i++
    else: A[k]=R[j], j++
disadvantages: Slower than Quick Sort in practice for small n

comparisons.quick_sort: Merge Sort is stable and always O(n log n) but uses O(n) space. Quick Sort uses O(log n) space but has O(n²) worst case.
comparisons.heap_sort: Both O(n log n) worst case. Merge Sort is stable, Heap Sort is in-place O(1) space.
comparisons.insertion_sort: Merge Sort dominates for large n. Insertion Sort faster for n < 20.

name: Quick Sort
definition: Quick Sort is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that selects a pivot element, partitions the array so elements smaller than pivot come before it and larger after, then recursively sorts both partitions. Average case O(n log n), worst case O(n²).
intuition: Choose any element as pivot. Put everything smaller on its left and larger on its right. That pivot is now in its final position. Recursively sort both sides.
steps: Choose pivot element (typically last element A[r])
For j = p to r-1: if A[j] <= pivot, increment i and swap A[i] with A[j]
Swap A[i+1] with pivot A[r] — pivot is now in correct position
pseudocode:
QUICKSORT(A, p, r):
  if p < r
    q = PARTITION(A, p, r)
    QUICKSORT(A, p, q-1)
    QUICKSORT(A, q+1, r)

PARTITION(A, p, r):
  x = A[r]  // pivot
  i = p - 1
  for j = p to r-1
    if A[j] <= x
      i = i + 1
      swap A[i] with A[j]
  swap A[i+1] with A[r]
  return i + 1

steps: Pivot 4 is at correct position index 3

time.worst: O(n²) — sorted or reverse sorted input

comparisons.merge_sort: Quick Sort uses O(log n) space vs O(n). Faster in practice but not stable and has O(n²) worst case.
comparisons.heap_sort: Heap Sort guarantees O(n log n) worst case. Quick Sort is faster in practice due to cache locality.
comparisons.insertion_sort: Quick Sort dominates for large n. Use Insertion Sort for n < 20.

name: Heap Sort
definition: Heap Sort uses a max-heap data structure to sort. It first builds a max-heap from the input array in O(n), then repeatedly extracts the maximum element placing it at the end, and restores heap property using MAX-HEAPIFY.
intuition: Build a max-heap where the root is always the largest. Swap root with last element, reduce heap size by 1, fix the heap, repeat.
steps: BUILD-MAX-HEAP(A): Convert array to max-heap in O(n)
Swap A[1] with A[i] — largest element goes to position i
pseudocode:
HEAPSORT(A):
  BUILD-MAX-HEAP(A)
  for i = A.length downto 2
    swap A[1] with A[i]
    A.heap-size = A.heap-size - 1
    MAX-HEAPIFY(A, 1)

MAX-HEAPIFY(A, i):
  l = LEFT(i) = 2i
  r = RIGHT(i) = 2i+1
  largest = i
  if l <= heap-size and A[l] > A[i]: largest = l
  if r <= heap-size and A[r] > A[largest]: largest = r
  if largest != i:
    swap A[i] with A[largest]
    MAX-HEAPIFY(A, largest)

complexity.recurrence: O(n) for BUILD-MAX-HEAP + n × O(log n) for extractions

disadvantages: Slower than Quick Sort in practice

name: Bubble Sort
definition: Bubble Sort repeatedly passes through the array comparing adjacent elements and swapping them if out of order. After each pass, the largest unsorted element bubbles to its correct position at the end.
steps: Outer loop: i from 0 to n-2
Inner loop: j from 0 to n-2-i
After each outer pass, A[n-1-i] is in final position
Optimization: if no swap in a pass, array is sorted — stop early
pseudocode:
BUBBLE-SORT(A):
  for i = 0 to A.length-2
    swapped = false
    for j = 0 to A.length-2-i
      if A[j] > A[j+1]
        swap A[j] with A[j+1]
        swapped = true
    if not swapped: break  // optimization
disadvantages: O(n²) — very slow for large inputs

name: Selection Sort
definition: Selection Sort repeatedly finds the minimum element from the unsorted portion and places it at the beginning of the unsorted portion. It makes exactly n-1 swaps regardless of input.
pseudocode:
SELECTION-SORT(A):
  for i = 0 to A.length-2
    min_idx = i
    for j = i+1 to A.length-1
      if A[j] < A[min_idx]: min_idx = j
    swap A[i] with A[min_idx]
advantages: Exactly n-1 swaps — good when writes are costly
disadvantages: Always O(n²) even on sorted array

name: Counting Sort
definition: Counting Sort is a non-comparison-based sorting algorithm that counts occurrences of each element value. It works in O(n+k) where k is the range of values. It beats the Ω(n log n) comparison lower bound by using element values directly.
steps: Find range k (maximum value in array)
Initialize count array C[0..k] with zeros
Count: for each element A[j], increment C[A[j]]
Accumulate: C[i] = C[i] + C[i-1] — now C[i] = number of elements ≤ i
Build output: place A[j] at position C[A[j]], then decrement C[A[j]]
pseudocode:
COUNTING-SORT(A, B, k):
  let C[0..k] = all zeros
  for j = 1 to A.length: C[A[j]] += 1
  for i = 1 to k: C[i] = C[i] + C[i-1]
  for j = A.length downto 1:
    B[C[A[j]]] = A[j]
    C[A[j]] -= 1
when_to_use: When k = O(n) — value range proportional to number of elements
advantages: Used as subroutine in Radix Sort
disadvantages: O(k) space — bad when k >> n

name: Radix Sort
definition: Radix Sort sorts integers by processing individual digits from least significant to most significant (LSD Radix Sort). It uses a stable sort (like Counting Sort) as a subroutine for each digit position.
steps: Find maximum number of digits d in any element
For each digit position i from 1 (least significant) to d (most significant):
Use stable sort (Counting Sort) to sort array by digit i
After d passes, array is fully sorted
pseudocode:
RADIX-SORT(A, d):
  for i = 1 to d
    use a stable sort to sort A on digit i
advantages: Linear time when d and k are constants

name: Bucket Sort
definition: Bucket Sort distributes elements into a fixed number of buckets, sorts each bucket individually (using Insertion Sort), and concatenates results. Expected O(n) time when input is uniformly distributed over [0,1).
steps: Distribute: put element A[i] in bucket floor(n * A[i])
Sort each non-empty bucket using Insertion Sort

time.worst: O(n²) all elements in one bucket

name: Binary Search
definition: Binary Search finds a target in a SORTED array by repeatedly halving the search space. Each comparison eliminates half the remaining candidates, giving O(log n) time.
intuition: Like finding a word in a dictionary — open to the middle, if your word comes before it search the left half, otherwise search the right half.
steps: If target < A[mid]: high = mid - 1 (search left half)
If target > A[mid]: low = mid + 1 (search right half)
Repeat until low > high (not found, return -1)
pseudocode:
BINARY-SEARCH(A, n, target):
  low = 0, high = n-1
  while low <= high
    mid = floor((low+high)/2)
    if A[mid] == target: return mid
    elif A[mid] < target: low = mid + 1
    else: high = mid - 1
  return -1

name: Asymptotic Notation
definition: Asymptotic notation characterizes algorithm running time growth as input size n increases, ignoring constants and lower-order terms. The five standard notations are: O (upper bound), Ω (lower bound), Θ (tight bound), o (strict upper), ω (strict lower).
intuition: We don't care if an algorithm takes 3n²+5n+2 steps — we just care it grows like n². Constants depend on hardware; growth rate is what matters for large inputs.

big_theta.meaning: Tight bound — f(n) is sandwiched between c₁g(n) and c₂g(n)
big_theta.formal: ∃ c₁,c₂,n₀>0: c₁g(n) ≤ f(n) ≤ c₂g(n) for all n≥n₀

big_o.meaning: Upper bound (worst case) — f(n) grows no faster than g(n)
big_o.formal: ∃ c,n₀>0: f(n) ≤ cg(n) for all n≥n₀

big_omega.meaning: Lower bound (best case) — f(n) grows at least as fast as g(n)
big_omega.formal: ∃ c,n₀>0: f(n) ≥ cg(n) for all n≥n₀

little_o.meaning: Strict upper bound — f grows STRICTLY slower than g

little_omega.meaning: Strict lower bound — f grows STRICTLY faster than g

name: Master Theorem
definition: The Master Theorem solves recurrences of the form T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n), where a≥1 and b>1, arising from divide-and-conquer algorithms. It compares f(n) with n^log_b(a) to determine the solution.
formula: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n), where a ≥ 1, b > 1
steps: Identify a (number of subproblems), b (size reduction factor), f(n) (combine cost)

cases.condition: f(n) = O(n^(log_b(a) - ε)) for some ε > 0

cases.meaning: Work is balanced at every level

cases.condition: f(n) = Ω(n^(log_b(a) + ε)) AND af(n/b) ≤ cf(n) for c<1

name: Dynamic Programming
definition: Dynamic Programming (DP) is an algorithm design technique for optimization problems with two properties: (1) Optimal Substructure — optimal solution contains optimal subproblem solutions, and (2) Overlapping Subproblems — same subproblems solved repeatedly. DP solves each subproblem once and stores results.
intuition: If computing F(5) requires F(4) and F(3), and F(4) also requires F(3) — why compute F(3) twice? Compute once, store, reuse.

two_conditions.optimal_substructure: Optimal solution to problem contains optimal solutions to subproblems. Prove by cut-and-paste argument.
two_conditions.overlapping_subproblems: Same subproblems solved repeatedly in naive recursion. DP stores results in table.

approaches.memoization: Top-down: solve recursively, cache (memoize) results. Natural recursive structure, lazy evaluation.
approaches.tabulation: Bottom-up: fill table iteratively from smallest subproblems to largest. Usually faster in practice.

steps: Identify optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems
Define the state: what parameters uniquely identify each subproblem
Build the solution and return dp[n]
classic_problems: Rod Cutting: maximize revenue from cutting rod of length n
Matrix Chain Multiplication: minimize scalar multiplications

example.result: F(10) = 55 in O(n) instead of O(2ⁿ)

comparisons.greedy: DP considers ALL choices, picks optimal. Greedy makes ONE local choice — faster but not always correct.
comparisons.divide_conquer: D&C: independent subproblems. DP: overlapping subproblems stored in table.

name: Greedy Algorithms
definition: Greedy algorithms make the locally optimal choice at each step, hoping to reach a global optimum. They require two properties: (1) Greedy Choice Property — a global optimum can be reached by local greedy choices, and (2) Optimal Substructure.
intuition: Always pick what looks best right now, without reconsidering. Like making change with coins — always use the largest denomination that fits.
greedy_choice_property: A globally optimal solution can be reached by making locally optimal greedy choices at each step.
steps: Prove the problem has optimal substructure
Prove greedy choice property holds (exchange argument)
Design the greedy rule (what to pick at each step)
Apply greedy choice, reduce to smaller subproblem
classic_problems: Activity Selection: select maximum non-overlapping activities
Huffman Coding: optimal prefix-free binary codes
Fractional Knapsack: maximize value with weight limit
Minimum Spanning Tree: Kruskal and Prim

example.greedy_rule: Always select activity with earliest finish time
example.intuition: Finishing early leaves maximum room for future activities

complexity.time: O(n log n) for activity selection (sorting)

comparisons.dynamic_programming: Greedy is faster but only correct when greedy choice property holds. DP always finds optimal.
comparisons.brute_force: Greedy is polynomial; brute force is exponential.

name: BFS (Breadth-First Search)
definition: BFS explores a graph level by level, visiting all vertices at distance k from source before any at distance k+1. Uses a FIFO queue. Computes shortest paths in unweighted graphs. Time: O(V+E).
intuition: Like ripples in a pond — explore all neighbors one hop away, then two hops, then three. Use a queue to process in order.
steps: Initialize: color all vertices WHITE, distance[v]=∞, predecessor[v]=NIL
While Q not empty: u = DEQUEUE(Q)
For each WHITE neighbor v of u: color=GRAY, distance[v]=distance[u]+1, predecessor[v]=u, ENQUEUE(v)

example.input: Graph: s connects to r,w. r connects to v. w connects to t,x.
example.output: Shortest distances from s to all reachable vertices

comparisons.dfs: BFS finds shortest paths (unweighted). DFS doesn't guarantee shortest paths but gives timestamps useful for topological sort and SCC.
comparisons.dijkstra: BFS for unweighted graphs. Dijkstra for weighted graphs with non-negative weights.

name: DFS (Depth-First Search)
definition: DFS explores a graph by going as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. Records discovery time d[v] and finish time f[v] for each vertex. Essential for topological sort, SCC, and cycle detection. Time: O(V+E).
intuition: Like exploring a maze — go as deep as possible, when stuck backtrack and try another path.
steps: Initialize all vertices WHITE, predecessor NIL, time=0
For each WHITE vertex u: call DFS-VISIT(G,u)
DFS-VISIT(u): color u GRAY, time++, d[u]=time
For each WHITE neighbor v of u: predecessor[v]=u, recurse DFS-VISIT(v)

edge_classification.tree_edge: v discovered from u — v is WHITE when (u,v) explored
edge_classification.back_edge: u→v where v is ancestor — v is GRAY. Indicates cycle!
edge_classification.forward_edge: u→v where v is descendant, already BLACK
edge_classification.cross_edge: All other edges — v is BLACK, not ancestor/descendant

comparisons.bfs: DFS uses stack/recursion. BFS uses queue. DFS better for topological sort. BFS better for shortest paths.

name: Dijkstra's Algorithm
definition: Dijkstra's Algorithm solves the single-source shortest paths problem on weighted directed graphs with NON-NEGATIVE edge weights. It greedily selects the nearest unvisited vertex and relaxes its outgoing edges using the RELAX operation.
intuition: Always expand the unvisited vertex with smallest known distance. Like GPS — always take the road that minimizes total distance so far.
steps: INITIALIZE: d[s]=0, d[v]=∞ for all v≠s, π[v]=NIL for all v
Add all vertices to min-priority queue Q
While Q not empty: u = EXTRACT-MIN(Q) — nearest unvisited vertex
Add u to settled set S
For each neighbor v of u: RELAX(u, v, w)
RELAX: if d[u]+w(u,v) < d[v]: update d[v]=d[u]+w(u,v), π[v]=u
correctness: Loop invariant: at start of each iteration, d[v]=δ(s,v) for all v in S
limitations: Does NOT work with negative edge weights

comparisons.bellman_ford: Dijkstra O((V+E)logV) but no negative edges. Bellman-Ford O(VE) handles negative edges.
comparisons.bfs: BFS for unweighted graphs. Dijkstra for weighted non-negative graphs.

name: Bellman-Ford Algorithm
definition: Bellman-Ford solves single-source shortest paths even with NEGATIVE edge weights. It relaxes ALL edges V-1 times. A V-th relaxation round indicates a negative-weight cycle. Time: O(VE).
steps: INITIALIZE: d[s]=0, d[v]=∞ for all v≠s
Repeat V-1 times: for each edge (u,v): RELAX(u,v,w)
For each edge (u,v): if d[v] > d[u]+w(u,v): NEGATIVE CYCLE DETECTED
Return d[] if no negative cycle
pseudocode:
BELLMAN-FORD(G, w, s):
  INITIALIZE-SINGLE-SOURCE(G, s)
  for i = 1 to |G.V|-1
    for each edge (u,v) in G.E
      RELAX(u, v, w)
  for each edge (u,v) in G.E
    if d[v] > d[u] + w(u,v)
      return FALSE  // negative cycle
  return TRUE
disadvantages: Slower than Dijkstra: O(VE) vs O((V+E)logV)

comparisons.dijkstra: Dijkstra is faster O((V+E)logV) but fails with negative edges. Use Bellman-Ford when negative edges exist.

name: Minimum Spanning Tree
definition: A Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) of a connected weighted undirected graph is a spanning tree with minimum total edge weight. Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms both find MST greedily using the Cut Property.
cut_property: For any cut (S, V-S), the minimum weight crossing edge belongs to some MST.

kruskal.approach: Edge-based greedy. Sort ALL edges by weight. Add edge if it doesn't create a cycle (Union-Find).
kruskal.complexity: O(E log E) = O(E log V)

prim.approach: Vertex-based greedy. Grow MST one vertex at a time, always add cheapest edge to non-tree vertex.
prim.complexity: O(E log V) with binary heap

comparisons.kruskal_vs_prim: Kruskal processes edges globally — better for sparse graphs. Prim grows from one vertex — better for dense graphs.

name: Divide and Conquer
definition: Divide and Conquer is an algorithm design paradigm with three steps: DIVIDE the problem into smaller subproblems, CONQUER by solving recursively, COMBINE solutions. Running time expressed as T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) solved by Master Theorem.

three_steps.divide: Break into smaller subproblems of the same type
three_steps.conquer: Solve recursively (base case: small enough to solve directly)
three_steps.combine: Merge subproblem solutions into solution for original problem

examples: Merge Sort: divide array, sort halves, merge
Binary Search: check middle, recurse on half

complexity.time: Solved by Master Theorem: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)
complexity.space: O(log n) to O(n) recursion stack

name: Topological Sort
definition: Topological Sort of a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) is a linear ordering of vertices such that for every edge (u,v), u appears before v. Implemented using DFS: insert each vertex at FRONT of list when it finishes.
steps: As each vertex u finishes (colored BLACK), insert u at FRONT of linked list
Return the linked list — it is the topological order
Graph must be a DAG (no cycles) for topological sort to exist
pseudocode:
TOPOLOGICAL-SORT(G):
  call DFS(G) to compute finish times f[v]
  as each vertex is finished, insert at front of linked list
  return the linked list

name: Red-Black Trees
definition: A Red-Black Tree is a BST with one extra bit per node (color: RED or BLACK) satisfying 5 properties that guarantee height O(log n), ensuring O(log n) for search, insert, and delete.
five_properties: Every node is RED or BLACK
If a node is RED, both its children are BLACK (no two consecutive red nodes)
All simple paths from any node to descendant NIL leaves have the same number of BLACK nodes (black-height)

operations.insert: BST insert + color RED + RB-INSERT-FIXUP to restore properties
operations.delete: BST delete + RB-DELETE-FIXUP to restore properties
operations.rotations: LEFT-ROTATE and RIGHT-ROTATE — O(1) pointer changes, preserve BST order

comparisons.regular_bst: Regular BST degenerates to O(n) on sorted input. RB Tree guarantees O(log n) always.
comparisons.avl_trees: AVL Trees are more strictly balanced (faster lookup) but RB Trees have faster insert/delete.

name: Binary Search Trees
definition: A Binary Search Tree (BST) is a binary tree where for every node x: all nodes in x's left subtree have keys ≤ x.key, and all nodes in x's right subtree have keys ≥ x.key. This property enables O(h) search, insert, and delete where h is tree height.
bst_property: For every node x: left-subtree keys ≤ x.key ≤ right-subtree keys

operations.search: Compare with root, recurse left or right. O(h)
operations.minimum: Follow left children to leftmost node. O(h)
operations.maximum: Follow right children to rightmost node. O(h)
operations.successor: If right subtree exists: minimum of right. Else: lowest ancestor with node in left subtree. O(h)
operations.insert: Search for position, insert as leaf. O(h)
operations.delete: Three cases: no child, one child, two children. O(h)

traversals.inorder: Left → Root → Right. Prints BST keys in SORTED ascending order. O(n)
traversals.preorder: Root → Left → Right. Used to copy tree. O(n)
traversals.postorder: Left → Right → Root. Used to delete tree. O(n)

name: Huffman Codes
definition: Huffman Coding creates optimal prefix-free binary codes for data compression. Frequent characters get shorter codes, rare characters get longer codes. Built using a greedy algorithm with a min-priority queue.
steps: Create leaf node for each character with its frequency
Insert all nodes into a min-priority queue
While queue has more than one node:
Extract two nodes x,y with minimum frequencies
Create new internal node z with freq[z] = freq[x] + freq[y]
Remaining node is Huffman tree root
Left edge = 0, Right edge = 1. Read code from root to leaf.

example.input: Characters: a(45), b(13), c(12), d(16), e(9), f(5) frequencies
example.output: a:0, c:100, b:101, f:1100, e:1101, d:111 — optimal prefix-free codes

complexity.time: O(n log n) — n extractions each O(log n)

optimality: Huffman code is provably optimal — minimizes expected code length

name: Priority Queue
definition: A Priority Queue is an abstract data structure supporting INSERT, MAXIMUM/MINIMUM, EXTRACT-MAX/MIN, and INCREASE/DECREASE-KEY operations. A max-heap implements it with all operations in O(log n) except MAXIMUM in O(1).

operations.EXTRACT-MAX: Remove root, replace with last, MAX-HEAPIFY. O(log n)
operations.INCREASE-KEY: Increase key value, bubble up. O(log n)
operations.INSERT: Add at end with -∞, then INCREASE-KEY. O(log n)

name: Lower Bounds for Sorting
definition: Any comparison-based sorting algorithm requires Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst case. Proved using the decision tree model: a binary tree where each internal node is a comparison. The tree must have at least n! leaves (one per permutation), requiring height ≥ log₂(n!) = Ω(n log n).

proof.model: Decision tree captures all comparisons — internal nodes are comparisons, leaves are orderings
proof.leaves: Must have ≥ n! leaves (one per permutation of n elements)
proof.height: Binary tree with ≥ n! leaves has height h ≥ log₂(n!) = Ω(n log n)
proof.conclusion: Any comparison sort makes Ω(n log n) comparisons worst case

why_linear_sorts_beat_it: Counting Sort, Radix Sort, Bucket Sort are NOT comparison-based — they use element values/digits, not comparisons, so Ω(n log n) bound doesn't apply.

name: Strassen's Algorithm
definition: Strassen's Algorithm multiplies two n×n matrices in O(n^2.81) time using only 7 recursive multiplications (instead of 8 in naive divide-and-conquer), reducing the exponent from 3 to log₂7 ≈ 2.81.
steps: Divide each n×n matrix into four n/2 × n/2 submatrices
Combine: C₁₁ = P₅+P₄-P₂+P₆, C₁₂ = P₁+P₂, C₂₁ = P₃+P₄, C₂₂ = P₁+P₅-P₃-P₇
